narrative:
The market remains in a business as usual regime, with the stress score sitting at 22 out of 100, firmly in calm territory. Broad systemic pressure is absent, and the modest five-day equity pullback of 2.5 percent, while slightly below trend at a z-score of minus 1.37, is not enough to shift the regime classification.

The VIX at 19.4 sits at the 56th percentile of its ten-year range, which is mildly elevated relative to the median but well short of any distress threshold, and the contango term structure confirms that options markets are not pricing near-term shock risk. Credit is offering no contradiction here: high yield spreads at 277 basis points stand at just the 13th percentile of their five-year range, and investment grade at 74 basis points is similarly compressed, both pointing to a credit environment that is supportive and broadly unconcerned with the equity softness.

The sector picture carries one note worth flagging: technology has shed 5.2 percent over five days, standing out as the clear drag while defensives have rotated into favour, with consumer staples up 3.9 percent and healthcare and real estate also gaining. This is a classic risk-off rotation within an otherwise calm market, suggesting positioning adjustment rather than systemic concern, but the magnitude of the technology underperformance relative to the broader index move warrants monitoring if it extends further.
